skill.name: firmware-analyst
skill.description: Guides authorized firmware security analysis for embedded and IoT devices. Covers firmware acquisition, extraction, file system review, binary analysis, emulation, and assessment reporting.
skill.summary: Guide authorized firmware extraction, emulation, and security assessment workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and credential patterns in SKILL.md. Most are markdown examples for authorized firmware analysis, but privileged chroot and dual-use payload guidance create elevated operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Firmware Emulation Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes sudo chroot commands for running firmware components. This is legitimate for emulation, but unsafe firmware could affect the host when executed with elevated privileges.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly use sudo chroot and execute a shell or firmware binary. Context shows legitimate emulation guidance, not confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Command Injection Payload Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents command injection patterns and payloads. They are presented as vulnerability examples, but can be misused against unauthorized targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The payloads are visible in a vulnerability education section. The nearby ethical guidance lowers confidence that the skill is malicious.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Tool Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill lists many extraction, analysis, compiler, and emulation commands. These commands are normal for firmware work, but they execute local tools on untrusted artifacts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static command findings are true as examples of shell tooling. They are domain-appropriate and not tied to exfiltration or persistence.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Network and Device Access Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL, local database address, serial device, and flash device paths are examples. They do not direct data to a suspicious endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholder vendor and localhost values. They fit normal firmware acquisition and emulation setup instructions.

low_findings.title: Credential Search Terms Are Audit Targets
low_findings.description: The password, API key, and private key strings are search terms for firmware review. No evidence shows the skill reading real secrets from the host environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The strings appear inside grep commands against extracted firmware files. There is no process environment access or network transmission.

low_findings.title: Vulnerability Keywords Are Descriptive
low_findings.description: Terms such as backdoor accounts and weak password hashing describe audit findings. They are not instructions to install backdoors or weaken cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The terms are listed under common vulnerability classes. The surrounding section explains what to detect during assessments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sudo Chroot Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Runs chroot under sudo for firmware emulation. This should require explicit user authorization and isolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly invoke sudo chroot. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the section is an emulation guide.

dangerous_patterns.title: System Command Injection Demonstration
dangerous_patterns.description: Shows command injection test payloads, including local identity and password-file examples. These are dual-use testing patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The payloads are explicit, but they are contained in a vulnerability class section. No target or automation is provided.

content.value_statement: Firmware reviews can miss embedded services, credentials, and unsafe update behavior. This skill provides structured extraction, emulation, and reporting steps for authorized assessments.
actual_capabilities: Outlines firmware acquisition paths from vendors, debug interfaces, and network services.
Guides file identification, entropy checks, string review, and filesystem extraction.
Lists common embedded filesystem extraction tools and when to use them.
Supports binary triage with architecture checks, disassembly tools, and checksec review.
Provides QEMU and Firmadyne emulation setup steps for extracted firmware.
Includes assessment checklists, reporting structure, and authorization guidance.
limitations: Does not include scripts or automation beyond documented command examples.
Requires authorized firmware samples and suitable local analysis tools.
Hardware extraction steps depend on device access, adapters, and analyst skill.
Emulation commands can be risky without isolation and host safeguards.

use_cases.title: IoT Product Security Review
use_cases.description: Assess router, camera, or appliance firmware for credentials, exposed services, and unsafe update behavior before release.

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty Firmware Triage
use_cases.description: Extract firmware, identify architecture, review web interfaces, and document reproducible findings within program scope.

use_cases.title: Embedded Systems Training Lab
use_cases.description: Teach students a structured workflow for firmware extraction, filesystem review, binary triage, and ethical reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Firmware Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized firmware review plan for this device type: [device]. Include required inputs, tools, safety checks, and report sections.

prompt_templates.title: Triage an Extracted Filesystem
prompt_templates.prompt: I extracted a firmware filesystem. Help me prioritize credential checks, service discovery, web interface review, and binary hardening checks.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Safe Emulation Steps
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe QEMU or Firmadyne emulation workflow for this firmware. Include isolation controls and verification steps before running services.

prompt_templates.title: Review Findings for Release Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these firmware findings for severity, exploitability, affected components, evidence quality, and remediation guidance: [findings].

output_examples.input: Plan an authorized assessment for a consumer router firmware image.
output: Scope and authorization checks for the target model and firmware version.
Acquisition and extraction plan using vendor image review and filesystem carving.
Prioritized checks for credentials, services, web handlers, update integrity, and binary hardening.
Report outline with evidence, severity, reproduction notes, and remediation steps.

output_examples.input: Summarize risks found in an extracted IoT filesystem.
output: A concise finding summary grouped by authentication, exposure, update security, and binary protections.
Recommended evidence for each issue, including affected paths, versions, and verification commands.
Practical remediation guidance for developers and product security teams.

best_practices: Confirm authorization, device ownership, and program scope before analyzing firmware.
Run unknown firmware tools and emulation inside isolated environments.
Document exact firmware versions, commands, evidence, and remediation guidance.
anti_patterns: Do not analyze devices or firmware without clear authorization.
Do not run untrusted firmware as root on a shared workstation.
Do not publish secrets or exploit details without coordinated disclosure approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help analyze?
faq.answer: It helps review embedded and IoT firmware for extraction, filesystem contents, binaries, services, and update security.

faq.question: Does it require special hardware?
faq.answer: Vendor firmware images may not need hardware. UART, JTAG, SPI, or chip-off methods require suitable adapters and experience.

faq.question: Can it run firmware automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance for emulation tools, but users must run tools in their own isolated environment.

faq.question: Is this only for security professionals?
faq.answer: It is best for authorized product security, research, bug bounty, training, or personal device analysis.

faq.question: How should risky findings be handled?
faq.answer: Record evidence, assess impact, avoid exposing secrets, and follow the owner or program disclosure process.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill contains dual-use commands and privileged emulation examples, although the stated purpose is authorized assessment.
